label: MySQL

schema_introspection.tables: SELECT table_name FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema = DATABASE() AND table_type = 'BASE TABLE' ORDER BY table_name
schema_introspection.columns_template: SELECT column_name, data_type, is_nullable FROM information_schema.columns WHERE table_schema = DATABASE() AND table_name = %s ORDER BY ordinal_position
schema_introspection.constraints_template: SELECT constraint_name, constraint_type FROM information_schema.table_constraints WHERE table_schema = DATABASE() AND table_name = %s

schema_detail_introspection.columns: information_schema.columns filtered by table_schema=DATABASE() and table_name=%s

notes: MySQL also accepts LIMIT offset, row_count syntax, but CoQuery should prefer the clearer LIMIT/OFFSET form.

joins.inner: SELECT ... FROM left_table INNER JOIN right_table ON ...
joins.left: SELECT ... FROM left_table LEFT JOIN right_table ON ...
joins.right: SELECT ... FROM left_table RIGHT JOIN right_table ON ...
joins.cross: SELECT ... FROM left_table CROSS JOIN right_table
notes: Avoid dialect-specific optimizer hints in generated baseline SQL.
Constraint enforcement and details can depend on storage engine and version.

upsert.template: INSERT INTO table_name (...) VALUES (...) ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE ...

safety_notes: CoQuery recognizes mysql:// but runtime execution is not implemented.
Use these rules for planning only until a MySQL proof slice exists.
